title: Attack Surface Change Trending
dataSources: `index=main` vuln scanner assets (`sourcetype=qualys*`, `nessus*`, `vuln:*`), optional `sourcetype=stash` nightly exposure summaries; `Intrusion_Detection` for correlated context
app: Splunk Enterprise Security, Qualys/Nessus/Rapid7 TAs, Splunk Add-on for Tenable
spl:
index=main (sourcetype="qualys:asset" OR sourcetype="tenable:nessus" OR sourcetype="vuln:asset")
| eval port_key=coalesce(dest_port, port, listening_port)
| eval svc_key=coalesce(protocol, "tcp") . ":" . port_key
| bin _time span=1d
| stats dc(svc_key) as distinct_services dc(host) as hosts_affected by _time
| timechart span=1w max(distinct_services) as peak_exposed_services max(hosts_affected) as hosts_with_exposure
description: Tracking newly exposed services and listening ports over time reveals shadow IT, misconfigurations, and scan gaps before attackers find them. Sudden increases in open ports or services often precede breaches or follow failed change control.
value: Tracking newly exposed services and listening ports over time reveals shadow IT, misconfigurations, and scan gaps before attackers find them. Sudden increases in open ports or services often precede breaches or follow failed change control.
implementation: Normalize host and port fields per your scanner TA; backfill a KV store of first-seen `(host, port, protocol)` tuples and flag deltas as “new exposure.” Run weekly after patch cycles. Exclude approved scanner ranges from “new” logic. Join high-risk ports to a lookup for prioritization. Schedule the search after nightly import jobs complete.
visualization: Line chart (distinct services and hosts over time), single value (week-over-week delta), table (top new port/protocol pairs).
knownFalsePositives: Attack surface can grow from planned scanning scope changes, new cloud assets, or a patch that finally reports accurately — trend and compare to change records before panic.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security, Qualys/Nessus/Rapid7 TAs, Splunk Add-on for Tenable.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=main` vuln scanner assets (`sourcetype=qualys*`, `nessus*`, `vuln:*`), optional `sourcetype=stash` nightly exposure summaries; `Intrusion_Detection` for correlated context.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Normalize host and port fields per your scanner TA; backfill a KV store of first-seen `(host, port, protocol)` tuples and flag deltas as “new exposure.” Run weekly after patch cycles. Exclude approved scanner ranges from “new” logic. Join high-risk ports to a lookup for prioritization. Schedule the search after nightly import jobs complete.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=main (sourcetype="qualys:asset" OR sourcetype="tenable:nessus" OR sourcetype="vuln:asset")
| eval port_key=coalesce(dest_port, port, listening_port)
| eval svc_key=coalesce(protocol, "tcp") . ":" . port_key
| bin _time span=1d
| stats dc(svc_key) as distinct_services dc(host) as hosts_affected by _time
| timechart span=1w max(distinct_services) as peak_exposed_services max(hosts_affected) as hosts_with_exposure
```

Understanding this SPL

**Attack Surface Change Trending** — Tracking newly exposed services and listening ports over time reveals shadow IT, misconfigurations, and scan gaps before attackers find them. Sudden increases in open ports or services often precede breaches or follow failed change control.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=main` vuln scanner assets (`sourcetype=qualys*`, `nessus*`, `vuln:*`), optional `sourcetype=stash` nightly exposure summaries; `Intrusion_Detection` for correlated context. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security, Qualys/Nessus/Rapid7 TAs, Splunk Add-on for Tenable. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: main; **sourcetype**: qualys:asset, tenable:nessus, vuln:asset. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=main, sourcetype="qualys:asset". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **port_key** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **svc_key** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1w** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare counts and new tuples to a **vulnerability or asset** export for the same week, and to one scanner’s own UI for a sample host and port set.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (distinct services and hosts over time), single value (week-over-week delta), table (top new port/protocol pairs).
grandmaExplanation: We track how exposed services and open paths change over time so surprise growth in our footprint is visible before an attacker maps it for us.
